title: Classic Houmous
description: Smooth, lemony, garlicky — the way it should be. The trick is whipping the tahini and lemon juice first.

steps.title: Drain the chickpeas
steps.content: Drain the tin of chickpeas but reserve a few tablespoons of the liquid (aquafaba) — useful if you'd rather loosen with that than water for extra body. For a noticeably smoother result, slip the chickpeas out of their skins by pinching them between your fingers; takes a few minutes but worth it.

steps.title: Whip the tahini base
steps.content: In a food processor, blitz the tahini with the lemon juice for about a minute until thick, pale, and creamy — it'll seize up and look almost like buttercream. This is the step that gives houmous its fluffy texture, so don't skip it.

steps.title: Add aromatics
steps.content: Add the garlic, ground cumin, and salt. Blitz again for 30 seconds to break the garlic down completely.

steps.title: Add the chickpeas
steps.content: Tip in the drained chickpeas and extra virgin olive oil. Blitz for 2-3 minutes, scraping down the sides once or twice. It'll look thick and gritty at first — keep going.

steps.title: Loosen and adjust
steps.content: With the motor running, drizzle in the ice-cold water a tablespoon at a time until the houmous turns smooth and silky and falls slowly from a spoon. Taste — it almost always wants more lemon, salt, or both. Serve in a shallow bowl with a swirl of olive oil on top.

notes: A few tweaks worth knowing: a pinch of bicarbonate of soda simmered with the chickpeas for 10 minutes before draining will break down the skins and give you restaurant-smooth houmous without the peeling faff. For a lighter, fluffier texture, blanch the garlic in the lemon juice for a minute first — takes the raw edge off. Toppings to play with: toasted pine nuts, sumac, Aleppo pepper, za'atar, or a spoon of harissa swirled through.
